sources.notes: Easy version of the online monotone monochrome matrix problem.
Official Round 1082 editorial used for the Ferrers/chain-graph invariant.
Cross-check source for the O(n+q) contribution invariant, also valid for the easy version.

annotation.summary: Cells of an initially white matrix are turned black online; after each insertion, decide whether row and column permutations can make the black cells monotone.
annotation.constraints: Easy version constraints are smaller than the hard version, but the same linear invariant works comfortably.
annotation.core_idea: A matrix is valid iff its black-cell bipartite graph is a chain graph, equivalently its rows can be ordered by nested neighborhoods into a Ferrers shape. Maintain the actual row-pair contribution and the canonical contribution induced by column heights. Equality of the two sums is necessary and sufficient for the Ferrers shape.
tricks: constant-time contribution update per black cell
compare actual and canonical row-pair counts

solution_variants.name: ferrers-contribution-invariant
solution_variants.summary: Maintain row-pair contribution of the actual matrix and the canonical Ferrers matrix determined by column heights; answer YES iff they match.

tags.evidence: The monotone matrix condition is exactly the chain-graph/Ferrers condition with nested row neighborhoods.
The online algorithm is based on updating row and column-height contribution sums after each inserted black cell.
Rows and columns must be sortable into a monotone Ferrers shape.
The implementation maintains row counts, column counts, and height frequencies under updates.
